steps.prompt: Drag the corners and watch the Sum
steps.subtitle: Reshape the triangle however you like. Keep an eye on the Sum of [a](blue), [b](orange), and [c](green) — does it ever move?

graphic.name: triangle-tear
graphic.caption: Rip the three corners off any paper triangle and line them up — they always settle into one straight line.

steps.body:
However you dragged it, the Sum stayed pinned at 180°. Here's the picture behind that: every triangle has three corner angles, [a](blue), [b](orange), and [c](green).
Tear the three corners off and lay them side by side, and they always make a straight line. A straight line is 180° — so [a](blue) + [b](orange) + [c](green) = 180°, every single time.
Let's use that, then meet the exterior angle hiding just outside each corner.

steps.subtitle: Two corners are fixed: [a](blue) = 70° and [b](orange) = 65°. Drag the slider to open [c](green) until all three add up to 180°.

feedback.incorrect: 70° and 65° already use up 135°. How much more gets you to 180°?

steps.revealText: 45° does it: 70° + 65° + 45° = 180°. The third angle is just whatever's left over from 180°.

steps.subtitle: A triangle's three interior angles always add to exactly 180° — no more, no less. Judge each claim against that.

cards.statement: 60°, 60°, 60° can be the three angles of a triangle.
A triangle can have two right angles.
cards.note: Two right angles are 90° + 90° = 180° on their own, leaving 0° for the third corner — so the triangle never closes.

cards.statement: 70°, 70°, 70° can be the three angles of a triangle.
cards.note: 70° + 70° + 70° = 210°, which is more than 180° — too much to close into a triangle.

cards.statement: 90°, 60°, 30° can be the three angles of a triangle.
If two angles are 40° and 75°, the third must be 65°.
cards.note: The third is whatever's left of 180°: 180° − 40° − 75° = 65°.

steps.doneNote: Locked in: a triangle's angles total 180° — no more, no less.

steps.body:
Extend one side straight past a corner and you swing open an [exterior angle](magenta) — like flipping a torn corner outward.
It equals the two far corners added together (the remote interior angles): [exterior angle](magenta) = [a](blue) + [b](orange) = 70° + 65° = 135°.
Why? Because [a](blue) + [b](orange) + c = 180°, and the [exterior angle](magenta) + c = 180° too — so the [exterior angle](magenta) must equal [a](blue) + [b](orange).

stages: Start with any triangle. Its three corner angles are the ones we want to add up.
Through the top corner, draw a line parallel to the base.
The two slanted sides act as transversals, so each base angle reappears at the top as an equal alternate interior angle.
Now the three angles sit side by side along that straight line, so together they make 180°.

steps.prompt: Find the mistake in this exterior-angle working
steps.subtitle: A student finds the exterior angle at corner C. One line is wrong — tap it.

lines.text: Angles a and b are 70 and 65, so c = 180 - 70 - 65 = 45.
The exterior angle at C is on a straight line with c.
So the exterior angle = a + b + c = 70 + 65 + 45 = 180.
So the exterior angle is 180 degrees.

steps.explanation: An [exterior angle](magenta) equals only the two remote interior angles — the two far corners — not all three. It should be [a](blue) + [b](orange) = 70° + 65° = 135°. (Adding c as well always gives 180°, which is a straight line, not a corner.)
steps.hint: An exterior angle pairs with just two of the three interior angles. Which one gets left out?
steps.doneNote: Right — leave c out. The exterior angle = a + b = 135°.

steps.prompt: Put it together: third angle, then exterior angle
steps.intro: A triangle has two corners of [a](blue) = 70° and [b](orange) = 65°. Work out the third corner [c](green), then the [exterior angle](magenta) at C — one move at a time.

parts.prompt: First add the two known corners: 70° + 65° = ?
parts.note: Those two corners use up 135° of the 180° total.

parts.prompt: The three corners total 180°. So the third corner [c](green) = 180° − 135° = ?
parts.hint: Subtract the 135° you just found from 180°.
parts.note: [c](green) = 45°, matching the slider you set earlier: 70° + 65° + 45° = 180°.

parts.prompt: To find the [exterior angle](magenta) at C, which two angles do you add?

options.label: The two remote interior angles, a and b
options.feedback: Right — an exterior angle equals the sum of the two far corners.

options.label: All three angles, a + b + c
options.feedback: That always gives 180° — a straight line, not the exterior angle. Leave c out.

options.label: The third angle c and one remote
options.feedback: No — c is the corner the exterior angle sits beside, so it's left out.

parts.note: The exterior angle equals the two remote interior angles — the far corners a and b.

parts.prompt: Now compute the [exterior angle](magenta) at C: 70° + 65° = ?
parts.hint: Add the two remote interior angles, a and b.
parts.note: The exterior angle is 135° — and notice it's also 180° − 45°, the straight line beside [c](green).

steps.doneNote: That's the full chain: third angle = 180° − (a + b) = 45°, and the exterior angle = a + b = 135°.
